title: Behavioral Patterns One by One — Quick Check

questions.q: What makes Command different from a plain function call, and why does that difference matter?
questions.a: A Command is a first-class object that encapsulates an action including its parameters and an undo() counterpart. Unlike a function call, a Command can be stored, queued, replayed, or undone — the action becomes a thing you can manage, not just a step you execute.

questions.q: What traceability cost does Observer introduce?
questions.a: Observer decouples subject from subscribers, which means control flow is no longer readable top-to-bottom. Tracing what happens after an event fires requires knowing all current subscribers, making debugging, failure propagation, and timing analysis harder.

questions.q: When does the State pattern become worth its overhead compared to a long if/elif chain?
questions.a: When the number of distinct behavioral states is large enough that adding a new state requires modifying many scattered conditionals. State pattern moves each state's behavior into its own object, so adding a new state means adding a new class with no changes to existing states.

questions.q: In a text editor, which pattern owns undo/redo, which owns propagating changes to the UI, and which owns mode switching (insert vs visual)?
questions.a: Command owns undo/redo via its history stack. Observer owns change propagation to UI components. State owns mode switching by delegating behavior to the current state object.
